sources.notes: Read the complete game definition, nonempty-remnant requirement, optimal-removal counting objective, and constraints n,q <= 1e5 and 0 <= a_i <= 50.
Read the full official tutorial and reference code; confirmed the zero-XOR Nim reduction, the at-most-two-kept-copies lemma, the value-frequency DP, tie counting, and the nonempty flag.
Actually read the complete __vector__ writeup; independently cross-checked the frequency compression, three keep-count transitions, and combinatorial multipliers.
Re-derived why removing two equal kept piles preserves XOR, why the resulting subset remains nonempty, and why counting kept index sets is bijective with counting removal sets.

annotation.summary: For every static range, keep a nonempty subset of its piles such that the resulting Nim position is losing for the first player, maximize the number of removed piles, and count all optimal removal sets.
annotation.constraints: n,q <= 1e5, 0 <= a_i <= 50, all range rounds are independent, and counts are modulo 998244353. The small 6-bit value domain permits a constant-size XOR DP, while the large query count requires O(1) range-frequency extraction per value.
annotation.core_idea: A Nim position is losing exactly when the XOR of its pile sizes is zero, so the problem becomes finding a minimum-size nonempty zero-XOR subset of indices. If value x occurs c times, an optimal subset keeps only 0, 1, or 2 copies: keeping more than 2 allows two equal copies to be removed without changing XOR. Build per-value prefix frequencies. For each query, run a DP over values x in [0,50], XOR masks in [0,63], and a nonempty flag. A state stores the maximum removed count and the number of ways attaining it. Keeping 0, 1, or 2 copies contributes respectively c, c-1, or c-2 removals and has 1, c, or C(c,2) index choices. The answer is the best state with XOR 0 and nonempty=true.
annotation.complexity: O(51*n + q*51*64) time and O(51*n + 51*64) memory.
tricks: Convert the game condition to a nonempty zero-XOR subset using the Nim losing criterion
At most two occurrences of each value need to remain because two equal values cancel under XOR
Optimize the removed count and accumulate counts only across tied optimal transitions
Use a nonempty-state bit to reject the illegal solution that removes every pile
Choosing kept indices and choosing their removed complement are in bijection

solution_variants.name: value-frequency-xor-dp
solution_variants.summary: Extract all 51 value frequencies for the query, then perform an objective-and-count DP over value, 6-bit XOR, and whether any pile has been kept, considering 0, 1, or 2 kept copies of each value.
solution_variants.complexity: O(51*64) time per query after O(51*n) preprocessing, with O(51*n) total memory.

tags.evidence: Each Nim heap has Grundy value equal to its size, and the first player loses exactly when the XOR of all kept heap Grundy values is zero; this is the decisive reduction.
The main algorithm is a DP over the 64 possible XOR masks, augmented with the processed-value index and a nonempty bit.
After the game-theory reduction, the exact optimization target is a minimum-cardinality nonempty subset of indices whose values XOR to zero.
Keeping one or two copies of a value with frequency c contributes c or C(c,2) choices, and tied optimal DP transitions are summed modulo 998244353.
A prefix-frequency table for each value in [0,50] supplies every query multiplicity in O(1), avoiding scanning the range.
